scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle writes from his perspective in 4th-century Athens, using local civic categories even when discussing constitutional theory.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle's floruit is traditionally centered around -348; his observations on state supervision reflect contemporary 4th-century political thought.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle's discussion of constitutional powers and 'polity' forms are framed by his experience with the Athenian and neighboring Greek systems.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348), as this rule describes contemporary Greek constitutional theory regarding the 'common people'.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle uses hereditary oligarchic controls as a contemporary counterpoint to the democratic systems he lived under.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348); the text analyzes contemporary forms of oligarchy prevalent in the 4th-century Greek world.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The description of courts drawn from 'all the citizens' is a hallmark of the Athenian democratic system where Aristotle resided.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348); judicial service for all citizens was an active feature of 4th-century Athenian democracy.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is analyzing the ideologies of his own time, specifically the democratic claims of equality common in the 4th-century Greek polis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348) as it reflects active democratic theory and practice during his lifetime.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle identifies 'The wealthy' as a contemporary political faction across Greek cities, including his own.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348); this represents his contemporary analysis of oligarchic political behavior.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Greek concept of 'noble' (eugeneia) and ancestral wealth were contemporary status markers in Aristotle's 4th-century environment.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348); these claims were part of the contemporary political discourse on status and rights.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly refers to a rule 'still compulsory' in the specific constitution of the city of Epidamnus.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle uses the present tense 'it is still compulsory' during his floruit (-348), indicating the rule is contemporary for him.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle explicitly names Athens and Argos as locations for this custom, using them as primary examples of contemporary Greek law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes ostracism as a current or recently practiced custom during his floruit (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle specifically discusses the recruitment practices (the muster-roll) of the Athenian army during the Peloponnesian War.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The author is describing a specific event (the war against Sparta) and a historical change in the citizen body that occurred around -431.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule regarding property-qualifications for office is explicitly attributed to the city of Ambracia.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes the gradual decay of this property rule as an example for his contemporary 4th-century audience (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle discusses the expulsion of groups specifically within the context of the colony of Thurii.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The founding of Thurii and the subsequent expulsion of the Sybarites happened around -443; Aristotle treats this as a historical case study.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerning the heiress (epikleros) is described as the cause of historical faction and war in Mytilene.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The revolt and capture of Mytilene by Paches occurred in -428; the rule described pertains to this specific historical period.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle specifically identifies this practice of expelling notables as part of the historical Megarian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes this as a past cause of revolution, but it fits within the context of his 4th-century political analysis (-348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The practice of liturgies (public services) was a staple of 4th-century Athenian democracy during Aristotle's residence.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348) as he is describing the contemporary methods democratic leaders use to extract wealth.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is discussing the evolution of modern democracy, likely referencing the radicalized Athenian model of his own time.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348); 'modern kind' of democracy refers to the systems contemporary to the author.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle's analysis of familial restrictions in office reflects the varied oligarchic and aristocratic rules found in 4th-century Greece.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348); he notes these rules exist 'in some places' currently.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerning the restriction of the Council of Elders is explicitly linked to the city of Elis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle uses Elis as a contemporary (or near-contemporary) उदाहरण of constitutional failure in his work (~ -348).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is theorizing on property qualifications in 'constitutional governments' (politeia), typical of 4th-century Greek cities.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (-348); this describes the operational mechanics of contemporary censitary systems.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text identifies a specific property qualification for 'honors' that existed and was then changed at Thurii.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes a past revolution at Thurii to inform his 4th-century audience about the dangers of high property bars (-348).
